studentData.projectTitle: Demonstrating SDN-Based Attack Isolation and Response in a Mininet VM
studentData.projectDesc: This capstone project utilizes the Mininet emulation platform and the OpenFlow protocol to demonstrate the effectiveness of Software-Defined Networking (SDN) in isolating cyber threats. By centralizing network intelligence, the project addresses the limitations of traditional hardware-based defenses, which often cause collateral damage to legitimate traffic during mitigation. The implementation was executed in two core phases: Phase 1 focused on the deployment of a virtualized network and the simulation of a high-volume UDP flood attack, while Phase 2 utilized real-time telemetry and manual flow-rule injection to neutralize the attack at the source switch. The results validate that a software-driven approach provides the fine-grained control necessary to preserve network availability for innocent users while maintaining a robust defensive posture against volumetric threats.
studentData.keywords: Mininet, OpenFlow, Software-Defined Networking (SDN)

studentData.projectTitle: Airflow Filtration Monitoring System Using Raspberry Pi and Differential Pressure Sensing
studentData.projectDesc: The Airflow Filtration Monitoring System (AFMS) is a Raspberry Pi–based prototype that measures differential pressure across a residential HVAC filter and translates the resulting data into simple operating states. The technical problem addressed by the project was the lack of a low-cost and practical method for determining whether a filter is operating normally or becoming restricted based on measured airflow conditions rather than calendar-based replacement or visual inspection alone. The implemented solution used a Raspberry Pi 4, a Sensirion SDP810-125Pa differential pressure sensor, pressure tubing, and Python-based monitoring logic to classify the HVAC system as SYSTEM OFF, OK, or FILTER RESTRICTED. Testing was performed on a residential forced-air system in three conditions: blower off, normal operation with the installed clean filter, and a simulated restricted condition created with an added dirty filter. The measured results were used to define prototype thresholds of below 5 Pa for SYSTEM OFF, 5–72 Pa for OK, and above 72 Pa for FILTER RESTRICTED. After the sensing and classification core was validated, the project was expanded into a full Core 3 phase that added a color-coded live interface, reduced-frequency CSV logging, daily summary reporting, a Streamlit dashboard, restricted-condition email alerts, direct Google Drive backup, and scheduled monitor automation using systemd timers.In its final form, the AFMS met and in several areas exceeded the original minimum viable goals. The prototype successfully demonstrated that differential pressure sensing can provide a practical maintenance signal for HVAC filter monitoring while remaining approachable, low cost, and educationally valuable. A proposed new extension phase is also defined in this report to show how the current prototype could be hardened for field deployment through a permanent enclosure, power-loss resilience, and broader validation across multiple HVAC systems.
studentData.keywords: Raspberry Pi, Differential pressure sensor, HVAC filter monitoring, Airflow restriction, Condition-based maintenance

studentData.projectTitle: Wearable LED Visor
studentData.projectDesc: The project as implemented uses LED matrix technology alongside an Arduino Nano to create a convincing facsimile of a fictional species for cosplay purposes. Project implementation will consist of two major core phases alongside one extension phase adding additional functionality to the final product. The project includes EET and CSC competencies and requires beginner soldering skills, including surface mount soldering as part of visor assembly.
studentData.keywords: LEDs, Arduino, SMT Soldering

studentData.projectTitle: Homemade VPN Using a DNS Server and Raspberry Pi 5
studentData.projectDesc: I have always used a VPN once I knew the dangers that could be lurking withing the vast annals of the internet, most people who do use a VPN like me purchase a service like McAfee, NordVPN, and many others that can assure safety and responsibility for your data. However, the thought came to me, why should I have to pay for a VPN when the option to make my own stands before me, I can save money, know what is happening and where my data is going all from the comfort of my own home. Thus, I set out to create my own VPN where by learning the process I can use Wire Guard, learn to configure my router to port forward, and create a dedicated DNS server all to fill the need of internet safety. So I set out and studied to create my capstone project, the creation of a VPN using a DNS server and port forwarding my router using a Raspberry Pi 5.
studentData.keywords: VPN, DNS server, Port forwarding, Raspberry Pi

studentData.projectTitle: Design and Implementation of a NAS-based Plex Media, Cloud Sync, and Backup Platform
studentData.projectDesc: This capstone project addressed the problem of fragmented digital storage and unmanaged personal media collections by designing and implementing a centralized Network Attached Storage (NAS) system built around a Synology DS620slim platform. The final system was configured with two Samsung 870 EVO 4 TB solid-state drives in Synology Hybrid Redundant Array of Independent Disks (RAID) or (SHR), which stands for Synology Hybrid RAID. The SHR is Synology’s own RAID management system that works similarly to traditional RAID, but it is designed to be more flexible and easier to expand, especially when using drives of different sizes, is formatted with the B-tree file system (Btrfs). The NAS was upgraded with 8 GB of additional Crucial DDR3 memory (RAM). The platform was used to host Plex Media Server for local and remote streaming, while also acting as a controlled archive for Microsoft OneDrive and Google Drive through one-way cloud synchronization. A major technical challenge emerged when remote Plex access failed because the internet service provider placed the network behind Carrier-Grade NAT (CGNAT). That issue was resolved by obtaining a public IPv4 address and validating the WAN path required for remote connectivity. The project demonstrates applied knowledge in storage architecture, networking, system administration, media service deployment, and troubleshooting. Although the external Western Digital (WD) 24 TB backup layer storage drive was not fully activated by the end of the project, the design path for Hyper Backup and a broader 3-2-1 protection strategy was established. Overall, the completed work produced a stable, scalable, and realistic home data infrastructure platform that supports media streaming, cloud data preservation, and future backup expansion.
studentData.keywords: Network Attached Storage (NAS), Plex Media Server, Synology, cloud synchronization, SHR, Btrfs, Carrier-Grade NAT (CGNAT)

studentData.projectTitle: Arduino MIDI Pedal Board
studentData.projectDesc: This capstone project addresses the challenge of creating a cost effective, customizable foot-controlled system for managing digital audio workstation (DAW) functions in live guitar performance. Traditional MIDI foot controllers are often expensive and lack flexibility for specific user workflows. The proposed solution is a DIY Arduino based MIDI pedal board that allows the user to control playback of backing tracks, record, and loop guitar audio, and provide LED feedback synchronized with DAW. The system integrates hardware (Arduino Leonardo, footswitches, LEDs) with software (Reaper DAW) and Arduino IDE to create a responsive and reliable control interface. The project demonstrates how low-cost electronics and MIDI communication can be combined to replicate and enhance commercial solutions. This project has been completed, and it is fully tested and functional.
studentData.keywords: Arduino, MIDI, Digital Audio Workstation (DAW)

studentData.projectTitle: PC Wi-Fi-Based Diagnostics Dashboard
studentData.projectDesc: This project involves the development of a dedicated, standalone hardware diagnostics monitor for a desktop PC using a Raspberry Pi and a 7-inch display. The proposed solution extracts real-time telemetry data (CPU/GPU temperatures, RAM, and Storage usage) from a Windows host machine running ‘LibreHardwareMonitor’ and transmits it over a secure ‘Tailscale’ VPN tunnel. The data is rendered on the Raspberry Pi using a custom-built Python/Tkinter dashboard featuring live PC diagnostics. This eliminates the need to sacrifice main-monitor screen real estate for system monitoring during resource-intensive applications, and the need to spend any additional resources to run the diagnostics.
studentData.keywords: Raspberry Pi, Python, Tkinter, LibreHardwareMonitor, Tailscale, Hardware diagnostics

studentData.projectTitle: Proxy Server for Game Hosting
studentData.projectDesc: For individuals looking to be able to play multiplayer games with their friends, it is not always easy. Many games offer server software that can be self-hosted from within one’s home, but the process required to set up and configure this server software is not always the most straightforward for the average user. The hardware requirements alone for self-hosting a game server tend to be a barrier to entry for this kind of multiplayer server as well. Because of this many gamers will pay a server hosting company to run the server for them using their compute resources. This project would aim to demonstrate how a game server hosting company could technically operate by having clients connect to a proxy server that can redirect users to their intended sub servers.
studentData.keywords: Networking, IP Addressing, Service Hosting, Proxy Servers, DNS

studentData.projectTitle: Raspberry Pi Smart Mirror (MagicMirror²) with LAN Remote Management
studentData.projectDesc: This capstone project includes the design, implementation, and testing of a Raspberry Pi-powered smart mirror that delivers an at-a-glance information dashboard through a two-way mirror and provides browser-based remote management over a local area network. The technical problem addressed is the lack of a consolidated, hands-free daily information display that can be maintained without requiring physical access to the embedded computer or monitor controls. The solution uses a Raspberry Pi 4 running MagicMirror² open-source software with modules for time and date, local weather, New York Times news headlines, U.S. holidays, and rotating daily quotes. A wall-mounted picture-frame enclosure houses a disassembled 27-inch monitor behind a tempered-glass two-way mirror. Remote administrative functions including system reboot, application restart, display power control, module toggling, and brightness adjustment-are provided by the MMM-Remote-Control module and accessible from any device connected to the same campus Wi-Fi network. Automated cron jobs manage a daily display schedule (on at 0630, off at 2130) and a nightly reboot for stability. The project was completed in three phases: physical assembly and baseline software (Core 1), remote management and automation (Core 2), and reliability hardening with documentation (Extension). Testing confirmed boot-to-dashboard times under one minute, consistent automation scheduling, and reliable remote-control functionality across multiple LAN devices. The total project cost was approximately $362.94.
studentData.keywords: Smart mirror, Raspberry Pi, MagicMirror², Remote management, Home automation

studentData.projectTitle: Solar Cell Arduino Clock
studentData.projectDesc: This project focuses on designing a solar-powered Arduino digital clock using a renewable energy source. The system integrates an Arduino microcontroller and an LCD display to maintain accurate time independently. Core phases include power system development and solar integration, while an extension adds user controls. The project demonstrates embedded systems and renewable energy applications.
studentData.keywords: Arduino, Solar, Digital Clock

studentData.projectTitle: NoPhish: A Browser-Based Anti-Phishing Extension
studentData.projectDesc: Phishing attacks remain one of the most common cybersecurity threats on the web, exploiting deceptive URLs and social engineering techniques to trick users into revealing sensitive information. Many users, especially non-technical individuals, struggle to identify malicious links before interacting with them, which increases the risk of credential theft and data compromise. This project, NoPhish, proposes a lightweight Firefox browser extension that analyzes user-submitted URLs and provides a real-time risk assessment score. The system uses rule-based logic to evaluate structural and behavioral characteristics of URLs, such as HTTPS usage, domain legitimacy, URL length, and suspicious patterns like IP-based addresses or excessive subdomain complexity. The extension also generates clear, user-friendly explanations to help users understand why a link may be considered safe or unsafe. The goal of NoPhish is to improve cybersecurity awareness and provide accessible, immediate phishing detection without requiring technical expertise or external system dependencies.
studentData.keywords: Phishing detection, Browser extension, Cybersecurity, URL analysis, Rule-based logic

studentData.projectTitle: Arduino Reaction Time Game
studentData.projectDesc:
The purpose of this project is to apply foundational concepts in electronics and programming to create an interactive game that measures two users’ response time to determine a winner. Specifically, my Arduino based reaction time game built using a breadboard. Throughout this progress phase, the focus has been on assembling core hardware components, including LEDs, push buttons, and a buzzer, and integrating them with Arduino code to produce accurate timing and feedback. Challenges encountered so far include debugging wiring inconsistencies, refining timing logic, and ensuring reliable user input detection. These troubleshooting issues have provided valuable opportunities for growth and understanding in both hardware and software systems. 
The timing measurements are functioning as intended, along with the push buttons, the buzzer, and the lights that signal the user when to go. The primary technical problem addressed In this project is designing and implementing a fully functional reaction time game using an Arduino and a breadboard based circuit. This involves coordinating both hardware and software to create an engaging and responsive system. Challenges included integrating multiple components such as LEDs, push buttons, and a buzzer, as well as developing accurate code that controls timing, user input, and game logic in a fun but seamless way. The proposed solution is to build a structured Arduino program that manages the sequence of events, triggering a visual cue, detecting a response from either user, then determining the winner by whoever pushed their button first. By combining organized wiring with efficient coding, the system is able to function as an interactive game that measures and displays reaction time performance.
studentData.keywords: Reaction game, Arduino

studentData.projectTitle: Design and Implementation of a Basic Home Network
studentData.projectDesc: This capstone project focuses on the design and implementation of a basic home network using both simulation and physical equipment. Many home networks are not properly configured, which can lead to slow performance, connection issues, and security risks. The problem addressed in this project is how to create a reliable, secure, and well-organized network that supports multiple devices. To solve this problem, the network was first designed and tested using Cisco Packet Tracer and then implemented using physical Cisco devices such as a router, switch, and wireless access point. The project is divided into three phases: Core 1 focuses on basic connectivity, Core 2 focuses on VLAN configuration for better organization and performance, and the Extension phase adds wireless connectivity and WPA2 security. The results show that the network successfully allows communication between devices while improving performance and security. This project demonstrates important networking concepts such as IP addressing, switching, VLANs, and wireless networking, and provides hands-on experience that can be applied in real-world IT environments.
studentData.keywords: Home network, Cisco Packet Tracer, VLAN, Wireless networking, Network security

studentData.projectTitle: Reppy: AI Native Field Operations Platform for Door to Door Sales Teams
studentData.projectDesc: Reppy is a mobile first Progressive Web App (PWA) designed for door to door sales representatives in verticals such as roofing, solar, and pest control. The platform addresses a significant gap in the sales technology market: existing Customer Relationship Management (CRM) software was built for office based managers, not for field reps working from their phones on doorsteps. Reppy provides an integrated suite of tools including real time door logging with address autocomplete, a live team map with color coded outcomes, team chat with Supabase Realtime, and a private AI coaching thread for each representative powered by Claude (Anthropic). The roofing specific tools include a Pre knock Briefing that fetches 12 months of real storm event data from the XWeather API and generates a neighborhood briefing using AI, and a Damage Scanner that analyzes roof photos using Claude vision to produce structured damage reports. The application is deployed on Cloudflare Pages and uses Supabase for authentication, database management, and real time subscriptions. This report documents the design decisions, implementation results, and technical challenges encountered during the full semester development of the project.
studentData.keywords: Progressive Web App, Door-to-door sales, AI sales tools, Supabase, Cloudflare Pages

studentData.projectTitle: Windows Defender Malware Alerts to iPhone using nfty
studentData.projectDesc: This project presents a system that converts Windows Defender security events into real-time push notifications using nfty. The goal was to improve malware alert visibility by notifying users instantly on a mobile device without manually checking system logs. The system uses Task Scheduler to monitor Windows Defender events and trigger notifications. Initial testing showed inconsistent results when relying only on Event ID 1116 (malware detection). This was resolved by implementing a custom XML filter to monitor multiple event IDs, including 1116, 1117(action taken), and 1118(action failed), improving reliability across different detection scenarios. The design was further simplified by using inline PowerShell commands instead of separate script files. Notifications were enhanced to include the computer name and username for better context.Testing with the EICAR file (commonly used malware triggering file) confirmed that alerts are triggered reliably, typically during file creation rather than execution. The final system is lightweight, cost-effective, and effective for real-time malware alerting.
studentData.keywords: Windows Defender, ntfy, push notifications, malware detection, Task Scheduler

studentData.projectTitle: Smart Home Network
studentData.projectDesc:
This project documents the design, optimization, and performance evaluation of a modern Smart Home Network built around a Verizon 5G modem-router and a Linksys Wi-Fi 6E mesh system with three nodes. The network integrates a diverse set of smart and IoT devices, including 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz solar security cameras, a Canon wireless AIO printer-scanner, an iMac, a Windows PC, two Amazon Alexa Dots, an Alexa-enabled air conditioner, four Alexa smart light switches, an iPad, an iPhone, a WD My Cloud NAS, and a Deeper Connect Mini for decentralized private networking (DPN). 
A key focus of the project is upgrading the network to DPN and supporting the new 5 GHz solar cameras while maintaining stability across all devices. Network tuning included optimizing mesh node placement, reducing channel overlap, segmenting IoT traffic, and mitigating RF interference between 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz devices. The results demonstrate that a properly configured mesh Wi-Fi 6E system can unify a large mixed-protocol smart home environment, minimize collisions, and deliver reliable performance for both high-bandwidth devices and low-power IoT components.
studentData.keywords: Wi-Fi mesh, IoT, Decentralized Private Networking (DPN), Mixed-protocol smart hom

studentData.projectTitle: Implementation of a Low-Cost, Scalable Voice over IP Communication System
studentData.projectDesc: This project addresses the technical challenge of establishing a working communication system by designing and implementing a low-cost, scalable Voice over IP (VoIP) system using the FreePBX platform. The proposed solution provides a stable communication framework by deploying a central PBX server that manages routing between different endpoints, including a physical Grandstream GXP1625 IP phone and 2 mobile Zoiper softphone clients. The implementation process covers configuration steps, such as managing SIP registration, optimizing network signaling, and ensuring consistent device connectivity through a simple bridged connection. The final system demonstrates that an enterprise-grade VOIP communication system can be achieved with minimal investment, offering a reliable and adaptable model for a small business, personal home setup or any situation in which digital communication is needed in a small scope.
studentData.keywords: VoIP, FreePBX, SIP, Telephony, Network Administration

studentData.projectTitle: Raspberry Pico + Pi home security system
studentData.projectDesc: This final capstone report documents the design and implementation of an affordable two-layer security prototype built with a Raspberry Pi 3 B+, a USB camera, MotionEye OS, a Raspberry Pi Pico 2 W, a PIR motion sensor, an LED, a buzzer, and a temporary disarm button. The problem addressed is the need for an accessible warning and deterrence layer for drawers, safes, or storage areas that may contain valuable or sensitive items. The first layer provides passive monitoring through a camera-based Raspberry Pi system configured for motion detection and email notification. The second layer provides active local deterrence through a Pico-controlled PIR sensor that activates an LED and buzzer when motion is detected. The extension adds a basic kill-switch/cooldown feature that allows the user to disarm the alarm temporarily so normal access does not cause constant false positives. The final implementation does not include the originally proposed AI detection and keypad password system, but it successfully demonstrates the major security theme: combining monitoring, alerting, and local deterrence into a low-cost prototype that can be improved in future versions.
studentData.keywords: Raspberry Pi, Raspberry Pico, Motion Eye OS, PIR sensor, Micro Python, Home security

studentData.projectTitle: Raspberry Pi Thermometer & Hygrometer: A Locally Hosted Environmental Monitor
studentData.projectDesc:
This project addresses the lack of affordable, locally hosted environmental monitoring systems that provide accurate and customizable temperature and humidity data. Many existing solutions rely on cloud-based platforms, which limit user control and data ownership. This capstone project develops a Raspberry Pi–based system that collects environmental data using a DHT22 sensor, stores it locally in a database, and displays it through a web interface on a local network. 
The system was successfully implemented and tested, demonstrating reliable data collection, storage, and real-time visualization. Results show that the system provides accurate environmental readings while maintaining low cost and full user control over data. The project emphasizes accessibility, security, and usability, while incorporating foundational networking and software development concepts.
studentData.keywords: Raspberry Pi, DHT22 sensor, Environmental monitoring, IoT, Local server

studentData.projectTitle: Pi-hole hosted on Free-Tier cloud resources
studentData.projectDesc: In my experience, ads and trackers are bothersome and intrusive, and VPNs that are free often come with downsides and lack of user control to compensate. My proposed solution is to create a setup in the cloud that can only be accessed through a VPN, in this case Wireguard. In this cloud stack, I will include Pi-hole, which acts as a DNS sinkhole which blocks requests towards domains/websites that service ads and trackers. It will also include Unbound, a recursive DNS server which reduces the reliance on third-party DNS servers. It uses docker containers in order to be as efficient as possible, and aims to be well within the limits of the free tier of any given cloud provider. For my project, the chosen provider is Oracle Cloud.
studentData.keywords: DNS, VPN, Containers, Cloud
